data.title: Wall Street Roundup: AI, IPOs, And Standout Earnings
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Oracle earnings stand out (0:25). Nebius, CoreWeave and the AI conversation (2:15). Circle Internet Group's IPO momentum (4:20). Lululemon earnings underline tariff complications (6:18). Docusign's now and future business (8:10). Musk, Trump, and Tesla (9:30). Homebuilders, consumer health insights, and upcoming Fed meetings (12:35).

Recorded on Thursday morning, June 12, 2025.

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our director of news at Seeking Alpha, welcome back to Wall Street Roundups.

Brian Stewart: Thanks. Great to be here.

RS: Always good to talk to you. At the near the end of the week, getting ready for a new week, a company that seems to be at the top of the list today is Oracle (ORCL), who just reported earnings. What did we see out of Oracle? What does that mean for the tech sector? What are your thoughts?

BS: Going into this week, Oracle was one of the few companies that was a highlight of the earnings schedules, pretty sparse this week.

So Oracle stands out just because there's not a lot else going on, but the results themselves are taken being taken by the market very strongly. Stock is up 14% last time I checked, recording this on Thursday.

Met expectation, issued strong guidance. Revenue growth in its cloud services sector, which is the one most affected by the AI innovations that the the company's putting in place, up 27% in the latest quarter.

This is an acceleration from the previous quarter. It's actually been growing. The growth rate has been accelerating the last several quarters, and the company in its guidance predicted 40% growth over fiscal 2026.

So even the accelerated growth that we've been seeing lately, they see accelerating even further. If you drill down to the infrastructure sector, it showed growth of about 50%, and it sees that growth rising as well.

In the earnings call after the report, company management's targeted growth rate above 70% for that part of the company. So innovations that the company is making are already affecting the the top line.

One concern coming out of it, the extent to which the increased CapEx spending that the company has to make to implement this AI strategy, how much is that going to cut into profitability?

So while the top line is obviously accelerating growth very quickly, there's worries that it might not follow the bottom line because they also have to increase spending.

RS: In terms of the AI conversation, another stock that has been talked about this week that is I think belongs as part of this conversation is Nebius (NBIS).

And we had James Foord talking about how he prefers this stock over CoreWeave (CRWV), which has had a lot of attention as a recent IPO and a lot of increased interest in that name.

Talk to us about why Nebius is being talked about right now, what they have going on, and how AI investors may be thinking about this stock, this company, and that part of the sector.

BS: Nebius has been attracting a very fervent investor base recently.

Just to give a little bit of background, the stock is up around 150% since April. It's up 35% so far in June. Its latest earnings report showed Q1 revenue growth of 385%.

So in a world of AI startups, the company's not quite a startup. It's got kind of an interesting history. But in terms of how we're talking about it, we can think about it as an AI start up because it basically refreshed its business plan a couple years ago.

The stock is showing results already. Like I said, revenue growth approaching 400%. Concerns about its high valuations, already rallied so much. It's already gotten so much attention that there's fear that it might have overshot its near term prospects.

Meanwhile, there's also a risk of dilution. The company recently raised funds. That process, if it keeps up, I mean, it certainly makes sense for management to take advantage of its higher stock price to build up its cash reserves, for further investment, and that process might end up diluting current investors.

So there's that concern overhanging the stock too. So whether or not the stock can push higher is really going to be a factor of what it says in as its earnings reports come out in the coming quarters.

RS: And speaking of huge leaps in momentum, we have another recent IPO, another in context of of the CoreWeave conversation, but another IPO in Circle Internet Group (CRCL).

What are you seeing out of them? Does that mean anything for the IPO market? Is that specific to what part of the sector they're playing in in the IT sector? What are your thoughts there? What are you seeing out of them?

BS: It came public last week. It was up 160% in that first first day. And it's continued to move higher over the past week. It's up four out of five days going into yesterday's close.

Priced its IPO with $31 per share, last week. It closed Wednesday at $117.20, so it's up about 278 percent from its IPO price. I think it's an interesting crypto play. So Circle is a stable coin company.

I think it's interesting in the sense that oftentimes we think about this Bitcoin (BTC-USD) especially, but crypto in general as being an alternative to the current financial structures, even to the point of replacing fiat currencies in some distant future.

But Circle really is the bridge between the two worlds. It points to a future where fiat currencies and crypto work side by side.

So I think that the interest in that shows that there's investor appetite for, I don't know how to frame it, the less aggressive crypto bulls, the ones who feel that it'll literally replace how we do business now.

I also think it's interesting Circle's business is tied to treasuries. The way that they, support their stablecoin is through holding short term treasuries. So, treasury yields will be an impact on the company's business.

So for investors, I think it's both a crypto play, but it's also kind of a bet on some of the more vanilla financial instruments that that we have in the market.

RS: Lululemon (LULU) reported earnings recently. What did you see out of them? Does that further any insight we have into the tariff conversations? Is it still too early to tell? What did you see out of them?

BS: I think Lululemon underlines the complications that remain on the tariff landscape.

So the stock was down 20% last Friday on its earnings. It's also continued to drift lower. If it finishes lower today, it was down when I checked earlier today, that'll be six sessions in a row that it fell.

This came as a company reported soft Q1 comparable results. The top and bottom line for the latest quarter were in line with expectations, but the comparable sales were soft.

Also, it cut its profit forecast. It cited tariff uncertainty. Also, analysts noted the rise in competition for it, and it puts Lululemon in a strange position.

One way that they can fight that increased competition is by cutting prices, but with the tariffs, that becomes difficult. In fact, the company might be tempted to raise prices to make up for the the higher tariffs.

On the earnings call, the CEO said that they're gonna look at price increases item by item, so taking it almost, to the individual apparel item that they're selling.

And they're looking to increase prices on a small portion of its its products, and those are meant to be modest in nature.

So you can see the company struggling to battle the higher costs that it's facing at the same time where it's facing competitive landscape with consumers having less funds available to spend.

RS: And we saw DocuSign's (DOCU) earnings also recently. Anything to mention there?

BS: DocuSign's another company, and, we'll probably talk about Tesla (TSLA). But we've talked about Tesla in the past as a company that has the now business and the future business.

And DocuSign's another company in that position, so they were down about 19% last Friday on earnings. That's after they lowered their full year billings guidance. Some analysts defended the company saying that this was really a renewal timing issue.

It was something that that management, one called it an unforced error on management, but fundamentally a near term issue that the company would work out over time.

Bulls look to the company's intelligent agreement management product, which is a AI driven way for businesses to manage their their contracts.

So it's a company where if you're a bull on it and you look at this as a a buying opportunity, it's recent decline, you're looking longer term as it rolls out these AI driven products that make business more efficient.

However, if you're bearish on on the company, you'd say it already got its COVID pop. It's already kind of built in that that customer base, and management clearly is having a little trouble just sort of the blocking and tackling in the near term.

So that might sap some confidence in management going forward.

RS: Let's get into Tesla. More back and forth, more changing of the current moment's reality. What did we see from Musk? What are your thoughts there? What did we see out of Tesla? What have you seen and how have you seen the market react to that? What's your sense there?

BS: So after the feud between Musk and Trump started to get pretty heated last week, we saw Tesla stock drop 14% last Thursday as that exchange heated up.

Recently, Musk has signaled a tactical retreat. He hasn't taken back everything he said. For instance, the critique he had of the spending bill that's making its way through Congress, he hasn't backed off of that, but he did say that he went too far with some of the other comments.

At one point, Musk had suggested impeachment for Trump. He's deleted that that tweet now. He was saying that he wanted to found a third party, which he seems to have backed off on that somewhat too.

All of Musk's businesses from Tesla but also SpaceX (SPACE), Starlink (STRLK) are heavily intertwined with government policy, that starts with actual contracts with government entities that those companies have, but it also points to just the regulatory environment.

So having an ongoing dispute with a sitting president who is certainly willing to use government powers to to fight a beef with individuals, just look at how Trump is handling the dispute with Harvard. I think Musk probably had people in his life warning him of potential problems ahead and convinced him to back down.

This comes ahead of a robo taxi launch in Austin on June 22. Wedbush's Dan Ives believes that the vast majority of the valuation upside for Tesla is in the autonomous future.

With that being a situation that requires a lot of communication with regulators, obviously, it's a business that, the government could shut down at any moment if they chose to, I think Musk probably made the business decision that it was better to be at least at peace with Trump if if not in the administration anymore.

RS: Anything else to point out in terms of upcoming earnings or previous earnings that we've seen recently that matter to you or to the market? What else from the earnings picture?

BS: Looking ahead, there's not much next week. It's kind of a strange week. The Juneteenth holiday is on Thursday, so markets are closed that day. It also, I think it's convinced a lot of companies to hold off on reporting earnings during a slow week. So you're basically getting a three day week, followed by a one day week for trading next week.

So, just in terms of themes, homebuilding is gonna be something to look for. Housing starts are coming out next Wednesday, but also Lennar (LEN), one of the the big players in the home building market, is coming out Monday. So we might get some information there.

Meanwhile, we see Kroger (KR) coming out later next week, CarMax (KMX). So these are stocks where we could get some some insight into the health of the consumer.

RS: And anything from the macro point of view? Anything else you would add to that part of the conversation?

BS: Just that the the big news next week is gonna come from the Fed. The Fed decision is on Wednesday. Currently, almost 100% chance, 99.7% chance baked in that there's gonna be no change in interest rates. So should be a pretty boring announcement as it goes.

Obviously, what's gonna be interesting is the way the Fed is signaling for the future. Currently, also broadly expected to be no change in July. There's another Fed meeting in July. 79.2% chance baked in, as we're speaking now. That changes as we get to September.

At that point, there's about 75% chance that there will be some rate change at that point. Only a 25% chance that things will still be the same in September.

So the the basic consensus is that we're gonna get a rate cut in September and maybe a couple more for the rest of the year. One note that's interesting is there are fractional chances of higher rates in the next few meetings.

All of them, for each meeting, it's below 1%. But it's the first time that's kinda come on the radar that there's a slight chance, it dissipates as things move forward. Like, if you look towards the end of the year, there's no chance currently baked into trading that there'd be higher rates.

But it's just an interesting note that's starting to be a blip on the radar.

data.title: Wipro Continues To See Client Ramp-Downs And Declining Revenue
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Wipro Limited (NYSE:WIT) previously reported Q1 2025 financial results, producing in-line earnings per share and a slight beat for top-line revenue.

I last analyzed Wipro in January 2025 with a sell outlook due to demand softness in Europe and the potential for generative AI disrupting its lower-value service offerings.

Those conditions have remained largely the same, with the company continuing to generate declining top-line revenue even though management has made cost containment adjustments.

I reiterate my sell outlook until the firm can begin to turn its fortunes around and meaningfully begin to grow top-line revenue.

Wipro’s Market And Approach

Wipro generates consulting, IT services, and business process outsourcing income from a variety of verticals and global regions, as the contribution percentage pie chart shows here:

Its primary service offering categories are:

  • IT services

  • Consulting

  • Business process outsourcing

  • Product engineering & design

  • Government services

  • Other

The 2024 worldwide market for software consulting was an estimated $350 billion and is projected to exceed $1.1 trillion by 2034, according to a Precedence Research market research report.

If accomplished, this growth rate would represent a relatively robust CAGR of 12.6% from 2025 to 2034.

The primary reasons for this expected growth are ongoing strong demand by companies for efficiency-related improvements to their software systems, and the SMB market is forecasted to grow at the highest rate of growth through 2034.

By region, North America represented the highest market share for service demand in 2023, as shown in the chart below:

Major competitors and other market participants include the following firms:

  • Accenture (ACN)

  • Atos SE (OTCPK:AEXAF)

  • Capgemini (OTCPK:CAPMF)

  • CGI Group 'CGI Inc.' (GIB)

  • Clearfind

  • Cognizant (CTSH)

  • Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu

  • Ernst & Young

  • Infosys Limited (INFY)

  • PricewaterhouseCoopers

  • International Business Machines (IBM)

  • Rapport IT

  • Oracle Corp. (ORCL)

  • SAP SE (SAP)

  • Others

WIT is active in other consulting and adjacent industries worldwide.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary characteristics:

Strengths

  • Diverse service offerings and geographic footprint, large client base.

Weaknesses

  • Reliance on N. American and European regions, elevated employee attrition rate.

Opportunities

  • Integrating generative AI in its practice areas, M&A deals to add expertise and footprint.

Threats

  • Intense competition amid client focus on cost-takeout work, currency fluctuations, attracting competent talent with AI competencies

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has continued to slide due to revenue softness in Europe and cost-takeout emphasis by clients. Operating income by quarter (red line) has improved slightly because of increased gross margin and flat SG&A costs.

The degree of operating leverage has also shown further weakness, due to a declining topline revenue trajectory.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has ticked higher as a result of improved productivity in fixed-price engagements. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have remained largely flat in recent quarters, so with lower topline revenue, the company is reducing SG&A spend accordingly.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have remained steady at four cents per share in recent quarters.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP)

For balance sheet results, WIT ended the quarter with $6.24 billion in cash, equivalents and short-term trading asset securities and $1.9 billion in total debt, of which a substantial $1.15 billion of debt is due within twelve months.

In the past 12 months, WIT’s stock price has risen by 8% vs. that of competitor Infosys Limited's gain of 4%, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a handy major financial and operating metrics table for Wipro, with forward and trailing twelve-month figures:

(Source: Seeking Alpha)

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was $1.8 billion, and capital expenditures were around $173 million.

The company paid $65 million in stock-based compensation, or SBC, in the last four quarters, so the free cash flow net of SBC was $1.75 billion.

The net dollar increase in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at only $0.3 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was only 0.6%, a very small amount.

So WIT has been very capital-efficient and shareholder-friendly in terms of its capital allocation policies, similar to competitor Infosys.

In mid-2024, Management characterized its plans to involve both dividends and stock buybacks, with a targeted payout ratio of 70% or more of net income over a three-year period starting in fiscal 2026.

Why I’m Bearish On Wipro

While management has reported improving bookings, it is also seeing increasing project delays, ramp-downs, and challenges in European demand.

It also faces continued pricing pressure from clients focusing on cost-takeout engagements and delaying or reducing transformational projects, such as from generative AI.

Management did not disclose its utilization rate, an important metric that indicates how much of its consultant time is spent on billable client work.

A top-tier consulting firm, like Accenture (ACN), has utilization rates into the upper 80s, so it’s a negative when the company doesn’t disclose that information, but rather just talks about how important it is.

I’ve prepared a graphic showing the frequency of various negative-oriented keywords in the most recent conference call with analysts:

The chart shows that the company is continuing to suffer revenue decline, which has not been helped by the tariff policy uncertainties among a wide variety of clients.

Also, the firm’s revenue mix is skewing toward cost-takeout and vendor consolidation work, which are subject to greater competitive situations, negatively impacting gross margins on those types of engagements.

As a result, WIT is going to continue to be under margin pressure and will need to generate additional efficiencies to maintain earnings and cash flow.

My DCF calculation for WIT is shown here, with the assumptions of $0.17 per share free cash flow, an 11% discount rate, and a terminal FCF growth rate of 12%:

The DCF suggests WIT may be fully valued at its current price of around $3.00 per share.

Given Wipro’s declining revenue profile amid ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties, cautious clients, and regional weakness in Europe, my outlook for the stock remains a bearish sell until we begin to see revenue growth resume.

data.title: HOMZ: This Housing Market ETF May Deserve More Attention
data.description:
HOMZ Fast Facts

The Hoya Capital Housing ETF (NYSEARCA:HOMZ) was launched on 3/19/2019 and tracks the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index, which is focused on the residential housing industry. HOMZ has 100 holdings, an expense ratio of 0.30% and a trailing 12-month yield of 2.46%. The fund pays distributions every month as they come from holdings, so they are variable. HOMZ has $33.55M in net assets and a 3-month average daily dollar trading volume of $125K. It is a small and not very liquid fund: it is important to always use limit orders, and it is not recommended to use it as a short-term trading instrument.

Strategy

As described in the prospectus, eligible stocks must meet liquidity and float requirements, and be issued by US-listed companies with significant operations in one of four segments: 1) Home Ownership and Rental Operations; 2) Home Building and Construction; 3) Home Improvement and Furnishings; and 4) Home Financing, Technology & Services. Each eligible company is assigned a classification based on the percentage of its revenues from each segment. Then, the largest companies by market capitalization are selected in each sub-segment. Index constituents are selected and weighted based on the table below.

The index was reconstituted semi-annually in June and December, and the portfolio turnover rate was 18% in the most recent fiscal year.

HOMZ Portfolio

HOMZ is exclusively invested in US companies and is classified by Morningstar in the Mid-Cap Value category. About 11% of asset value is in large companies, 40% in mid-caps, 29% in small caps and 19% in micro caps. The next chart shows the sector breakdown based on GICS classification. The portfolio is focused on the real estate and consumer discretionary sectors (39.6% and 31% of assets, respectively), and also has significant exposure to financials (17.5%). At the sub-sector level, residential REITs (24.6%) and household durables (22%) come in first and second positions.

The portfolio is quite diversified and risks related to individual companies are low. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 18% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 2.9%.

Although HOMZ is a good instrument to gain exposure to the housing market, the underlying market trend is not very favorable for now. After falling back from a 16-year high reached in April 2022, Housing Starts have been going sideways for two years.

The Hoya Capital Housing ETF (HOMZ) offers exposure to the housing market with a portfolio of 100 stocks focused on real estate and consumer discretionary. HOMZ is well diversified in holdings and has value characteristics. HOMZ's performance since inception is superior to benchmarks in residential real estate, consumer discretionary, and mid-caps, although it has lagged recently. HOMZ looks like an excellent instrument to gain exposure to the housing market, but investors should also consider its drawbacks: low assets and liquidity, and uncertain underlying market trends.

data.title: Novavax: New Vaccine, Same Old Story - Still A Sell
data.description:
Investment Overview

My last two notes on Novavax, Inc. (NASDAQ:NVAX) shared with Seeking Alpha readers, the first in October last year (2024), and the second at the end of February this year (2025), have both been "Sell" recommendations.

Shares are down >25% since my first note, and 14% since my second, reflecting my thesis that investors should expect a slow decline in the company's valuation. At the time of writing, Novavax stock is worth $7.2 per share, and has a market cap of $1.2bn. I continue to think this is too high.

As most readers may be aware (and as I covered in my last note), Novavax, with the help of $1.6bn funding from the U.S. government in 2020, created a COVID vaccine, Nuvaxovid, which was as effective and safe as those created by Moderna (MRNA) and Pfizer (PFE) / BioNTech (BNTX), but while these companies booked close to $150bn in revenues, and tens of billions in profits, Novavax struggled to secure approvals in key markets and ended up making a $(418m) loss in 2020, $(1.74bn) loss in 2021, $(658m) in 2022, an $(545m) in 2023.

Its shares, which had traded >$290 per share in 2021, quickly fell back to earth, and have traded <$10 since the beginning of 2023. In 2024, French Pharma giant Sanofi (SNY) came to the company's rescue, paying the company $500m up-front to gain the rights to market and sell Nuvaxovid in most territories after 2025, and to use Novavax' technology to develop a COVID / Flu combo vaccine, and its Matrix-M adjuvant technology, in exchange for milestone payments and royalties on sales.

Personally, I suspect it may have been better for all parties if Sanofi had simply acquired Novavax outright, to avoid a confusing situation in which both companies can continue to develop vaccines using Novavax' vaccine technology platform, and involving an equally confusing array of milestone payments and royalties, while permitting Novavax to license its technology to other Pharma companies.

As a reminder, here is a description of that vaccine technology, as per Novavax' 2024 annual report / 10K submission:

Once a target of interest has been identified, the genetic sequence encoding an antigen is selected for developing the vaccine construct. The genetic sequence may be optimized to enhance protein stability or confer resistance to degradation. This genetic construct is inserted into the baculovirus Spodoptera frugiperda (“Sf-/BV”) insect cell-expression system, which enables efficient, large-scale expression of the optimized protein. The Sf-/BV system produces protein-based antigens that are properly folded and modified, which can be critical for functional, protective immunity. Our testing shows this results in a highly immunogenic nanoparticle that is ready to be formulated with Matrix-M™ adjuvant.

Novavax' Bewildering Q1 Earnings & Financial Outlook Explained

In Q1 2025, Novavax reported an impressive - on the face of it - set of Q1 earnings. Revenues came to $667m, but we should bear in mind Novavax' statement that:

Higher product sales for the first quarter of 2025 were primarily due to $603 million of revenue recognized with the termination of two Advance Purchase Agreements ("APA") and related to cash received in prior years.

According to Novavax' Q1 2025 quarterly report / 10Q submission, $555.7m of the $603m figure was related to the termination of an APA with Canada, owing to its' "not receiving regulatory approval for its COVID-19 Vaccine using bulk antigen produced at Biologics Manufacturing Centre Inc. on or before December 31, 2024". The other APA terminated was with New Zealand.

My understanding is there are therefore no more revenues due from these sources, and that the substantial revenue figure should be considered a "one-off."

Here is the full-year guidance Novavax provided when announcing Q1 earnings:

The "adjusted licensing, royalties and other revenue figure includes a $175m milestone payment from Sanofi to Novavax upon approval of its Biologics License Application ("BLA"), "and two separate $25 million milestone payments upon the transfer to Sanofi of the Marketing Authorizations for the U.S. and EU markets, respectively." Again, these are one-off payments.

The positive news is that on May 19th, the FDA finally approved Nuvaxovid in the U.S. (it had received "emergency use authorisation" before, but never a full approval), which presumably ensures Novavax will receive the stated milestone payments.

Novavax handed over rights to lead sales and marketing activities to Sanofi in 2025, however, so is now only entitled to "ongoing tiered royalties from stand-alone COVID-19 vaccine sales."

That explains why the Sanofi section of the guidance section above had been left blank - the Pharma has not yet provided any guidance on revenue expectations for the vaccine.

Novavax says it it will earn royalties in the "high teens to low twenties" on net product sales of Nuvaxovid, and as as we can see in the table below, if Sanofi initiates a pivotal program for a COVID/Flu vaccine, and completes a first commercial sale, there could another $350m due to Novavax. (Source: Novavax May 2025 corporate presentation).

Can Novavax Earn Much More From Sanofi Deal?

It's been well publicized that the Trump administration has appointed figures regarded as vaccine sceptics to key roles in government healthcare administration. For example, Secretary Robert F Kennedy to the role of head of the Department for Health & Human Services ("HHS"), Dr Marty Makary to lead the FDA, and Dr Vinay Prasad as head of the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research ("CBER").

Secretary Kennedy recently removed all eighteen members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices ("ACIP"), and made eight new appointments, some of who are also regarded as vaccine sceptics.

Arguably, then, the outlook for the endemic COVID vaccination market is uncertain, at best, and given Sanofi is an overseas company that may have to pay tariffs if importing vaccine product into the U.S., the Pharma may already be downgrading its internal expectations.

Moderna (MRNA) once speculated a private US Covid market could be worth nearly $13bn per annum. Let's halve that figure, based on recent developments, and then assume Sanofi grabs a 10% share of that market, and pays 20% royalties to Novavax. In that scenario, Novovax would earn ~$130m per annum.

There is a chance Sanofi succeeds with a COVID flu vaccine, but if it does, while Novavax may receive $350m in milestone payments, it earns a lower royalty percentage, plus, that product would likely cannibalise sales of Nuvaxovid, so I'd speculate its royalty revenues would be unlikely to exceed $150m. If we include other territories, we might raise that figure to $200m per annum.

Novavax points to the fact studies show patients experienced 39% fewer symptoms compared to MRNA vaccines, and Sanofi's flu vaccine earned $2.7bn, so if you are looking for grounds for optimism, there are some to be found - personally, I find more grounds for pessimism, as explained above.

Novavax - Can Other Projects & Partners Help Company Complete Turnaround?

Since 2023, Novavax has reduced its headcount by ~80%, the CEO told the audience during a presentation given at the Goldman Sachs (GS) Healthcare conference yesterday.

The company may make a profit this year, but when we consider it is forecasting for combined R&D and SG&A expenses of $475 - $525m, and that there may be only $75m of milestone payments due in 2026, plus let's speculate $150m in royalty revenues, a heavy loss may be reported in 2026 unless costs are slashed. The company advises costs may be reduced to ~$250m by 2027, but will it be able to find other source of revenues?

Novavax has produced a successful Malaria vaccine in partnership with the University of Oxford, and the Serum Institute of India - a notable and praiseworthy achievement - although apparently, not one that will be a source of meaningful revenues.

The company says there is "significant and growing interest in Matrix-M and our technology platform," and says that investors may expect "potential additional BD announcements" in 2025, however I would regard this as somewhat vague, and therefore only the faintest of "buy" signals.

Novavax also says it is "advancing early-stage an preclinical programs in high-value, vaccine preventable diseases," and suggests it has a COVID / Flu vaccine and influenza vaccine in late stage clinical development, while other programs remain preclinical.

It is hard to imagine Novavax beating Sanofi - or indeed Moderna - to market with a combo vaccine, while its influenza vaccine seems to have been in development for decades.

Concluding Thoughts - Novavax New Approach Impresses, But I Foresee Further Declines

I must admit that I am relatively impressed by the presentation Novavax released in May, and will emphasize once again that the company has developed an effective, safe vaccine, but that vaccine is not in the hands of Sanofi, it has never been a profitable product, and presently, vaccine makers are under extreme pressure.

It's simply not a recipe for success, and again, I'd question why Sanofi and Novavax made such a bizarre and complex agreement over Nuvaxovid and future product development, and why Sanofi didn't simply acquire Novavax outright.

I have said before Novavax' best plan may be to passively accept royalty streams from Sanofi, not over-invest in internal R&D, and see if it can outsource its Matrix-M adjuvant to interested parties.

I note, however, that since the end of the pandemic, Dynavax (DVAX), which is also in possession of an adjuvant that earned >$500m revenues for its adjuvant CPG-1018 during 2022, used in certain COVID vaccines, has booked no revenue for CPG-1018 since.

It's questionable whether a COVID / flue combo vaccine will ever see the light of day, and how strong Sanofi's commitment to developing one truly is, but the logic, at least, is sound.

There are positive elements to the Novavax value proposition, but in my view, they simply don't add up to a >$1bn valuation. This is a company that is downsizing, that has booked practically all the revenues it can from its legacy pandemic era, has a few hundred million it may (or may not) claim from partner Sanofi in milestones over the next few years, and otherwise, one source of revenues - royalties from a COVID vaccine whose earning potential is highly uncertain.

For me, that amounts to a ~$500m valuation, not >$1bn. Although I can see low triple-digit million revenue potential in an optimistic scenario, I can't see profitability occurring in 2026, and likely not in 2027 either. Such dynamics are likely to drag down the share price and valuation to a more appropriate level.

data.title: SCHM: Falling Behind Other Mid-Cap Options
data.description:
Last month, I wrote about the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrat ETF (REGL) and its conservative, dividend-focused approach to participating in the mid-cap space. While the fund only captures 87% of the gains of the broader mid-cap index, the fund's benefit can be seen in participating in only 77% of its losses. For much of that analysis, I compared REGL to the much larger SPDR® S&P MIDCAP 400 ETF Trust (MDY).

For this analysis, I want to look at another one of the more popular mid-cap funds, the Schwab U.S. Mid-Cap ETF™ (NYSEARCA:SCHM). I think that the mid-cap sector is often overlooked. Investors who want to diversify outside of the S&P 500 will often skip over mid-caps in favor of the allure of rapid appreciation with small caps. However, history has shown that the mid-cap space has outperformed both large and small sectors for extended periods of time.

Based on the research I have conducted, I do not believe that SCHM is the best option for investing in mid-cap stock. It appears that both REGL and MDY are better options than this Schwab ETF. While SCHM does have some positive attributes, I am not recommending investors start a new position in the fund at the current time.

ETF Description and Past Performance

The Schwab U.S. Mid-Cap ETF™ is a passively managed fund from Charles Schwab that aims to track the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Mid-Cap Total Stock Market Index. This index includes the companies ranked 501 to 1,000 in the Dow Jones Total Market Index and is weighted by the float adjusted market cap of its holdings. SCHM has $11.42 billion assets under management for a portfolio that contains 498 individual stocks.

Below is a list of the fund's top 10 holdings as of June 12. Robinhood Markets (HOOD) is the only stock that makes up more than 0.75% of the portfolio. All stocks that fall outside of the top 10 represent no more than 0.49% of the fund. Diversification is a definite strength of SCHM.

Below is the breakdown of the ETF by sector and market cap allocation. Over a third of SCHM is represented by industrials and financials, with industrials taking the top spot at just over 19% of the fund. Consumer discretionary and technology are the only other two sectors that comprise at least 10% of the portfolio.

There are various definitions of exactly what constraints should be placed on market caps to define a mid-cap stock. Investopedia uses a cap range of $2 billion to $10 billion. Other definitions break down stocks by percentile rankings within the entire market. Morningstar refers to large-cap stocks as making up the top 70% of the total U.S. market. Mid-caps take the next 20% in this definition. Small-caps and micro-caps take up the final 7% and 3%, respectively.

SCHM and the index it follows use a different breaking point of $15 billion as the differentiator between large and mid-caps. Using their definition, large-cap stocks compose 21.45% of the Schwab fund, with mid-caps taking up almost the entirety of the balance. The weighted average market cap of this ETF is $12.5 billion.

The fund's historical returns have been on a gradual decline pattern over the last five years. SCHM was in the top quartile of other mid-cap funds in 2017 and 2018, but it has been in the third or fourth quartile in three of the last four years. SCHM has also underperformed its index on Morningstar for five of the last six years, including a discrepancy of almost 3.5% last year. In 2025, the fund is down just over 0.5%, once again trailing its index and putting it in Morningstar's third quartile for its category.

The fund has an exceptionally low expense ratio of 0.04%. SCHM currently yields 1.41% with a 10-year average dividend growth rate of 7.23%. Payouts are made four times each year at the end of each quarter.

ETF Analysis

I am a firm believer that a mid-cap ETF can play an important role in a diversified investment portfolio. SCHM certainly checks a lot of boxes at first glance, with the strong reputation of Schwab and its management fee of only four basis points. Lower fees should increase the odds of higher returns over the long haul.

From my point of view, the best way to analyze this fund is to compare it to some of its alternatives. For this analysis, I will use REGL and MDY. In addition to the article that I wrote about REGL last month, investors may want to check out a breakdown of the positive attributes for MDY from Michael Gayed that was also written in May of this year.

Below is a chart that contrasts several key financial metrics from all three funds. For the source of this data, I relied on both the funds' fact sheets and Morningstar. All three ETFs are passively managed and follow their own unique indices. I expected to see some noticeable differences in the ProShares fund because it follows a unique sub-sector of dividend aristocrats from a larger mid-cap index. However, I was surprised by the gaps in data between SCHM and MDY.

SCHM

The stocks in the Schwab mid-cap fund trade at a higher PE ratio, price-to-book ratio, and price-to-cash flow ratio. SCHM also finished last in long-term earnings growth expectations, so there is no apparent advantage to paying up for the higher multiples. The Schwab fund does finish second in dividend yield and has the lowest expense fee.

Without looking at the individual stocks or sectors that are contained in each fund, REGL and MDY look like clear winners. It should be noted that REGL has no fund allocation in the tech sector, which explains part of its underperformance when the overall market and higher PE growth stocks are doing well.

Mid-Caps Should Outperform, SCHM Has Not

As part of its fund data to promote its Dividend Aristocrat ETF, ProShares gives the following justification for investing in the mid-cap space:

Since July of 1991, the S&P MidCap 400 has outperformed both large-cap and small-cap stocks, and done so consistently, beating the S&P 500 in over 75% of rolling 10-year periods.

ProShares refers to mid-cap investing as the "sweet spot" for domestic stocks. The sector has returned almost 12% in compound average returns in the last 24 years. The S&P 500 is about 110 basis points behind, with small-caps even farther back.

According to State Street, mid-cap stocks have finished the calendar year with higher returns than large-caps 48% of the time since 1994. For small-caps, the outperformance happens 54% of the time. However, there appears to be no recent time period when Schwab US Mid-Cap ETF has been able to make use of this potential advantage. The ETF trails the returns of the S&P 500 in every time period, from just the first five and a half months of this year, all the way back to a 10-year time span.

Most of the blame for this differentiation between these funds has to fall back on the indices that they follow. Nonetheless, it is clear that there are meaningful advantages to both REGL and MDY. Investors who are looking to allocate funds with capital growth in mind should favor MDY, while those who want a slightly more conservative approach with the opportunity for greater income should consider REGL.

Risks to Consider

Because mid-cap companies do not have access to the same financial resources as large-cap stocks, they may be more volatile during periods of challenging economic times. Higher inflation, unemployment, and a contraction in U.S. GDP would all impact SCHM in a meaningful way. Should the U.S. economy enter a recession later this year, this fund, as well as the two other mid-cap funds discussed here, would likely decrease in value.

JPMorgan recently reduced its odds for a recession in the U.S. to 40% from 60%, but there is still a great deal of uncertainty ahead that could increase those odds again. Trade conflicts, military tension in the Middle East, and the results of recent Congressional budget debates could all impact the economic outlook for the remainder of this calendar year.

Final Thoughts

At the current time, I do not see a compelling reason to buy this fund, and Seeking Alpha's Quant rating system appears to confirm my assessment. This ETF certainly deserves its "A+" rating in expenses, but the low fees have not done enough to help with the fund's overall performance. While the SA Quant rating is in the Hold category, it is on the low end of that range with a score of 2.54. It is interesting to note in the history graph below that the fund has even spent a good bit of its time this year rated as a Sell.

Investors who are looking for a way to add exposure to mid-cap stocks would be better served by taking a longer look at the other two mid-cap funds discussed in this article.

The Fund exhibited strong relative performance compared to its primary index, the S&P MidCap 400 Growth Index. The operational performances of the Fund's holdings have been robust, and if this strength continues, it should drive wealth creation over the long term. In the short term, stock prices can fluctuate based on macroeconomic sentiment rather than business fundamentals. The first quarter ended with the worst investor sentiment since the early days of COVID, driven by uncertainty surrounding the tariffs announced by the US on April 2nd. The future remains uncertain, and it is unclear whether these tariffs will remain in place or if they are just a starting point in negotiations. That said, the Fund's holdings are likely well-suited to weather a high-tariff environment and possible trade wars that result.

Notably, 57% of the Fund is invested in international companies, with only one directly exposed to US tariffs. Hermes, which generates 15% of its revenue from the Americas, has customers who can easily absorb tariff-induced price increases.

The Fund's managers seek to invest in companies with durable, forecastable, and growing earnings. Such companies often have strong competitive advantages that should help them weather tariff-induced economic challenges. Detailed below are several of the advantages that our Fund holdings may benefit from.

Alternative Asset Managers ("Alt. Managers"): Alt. Managers generate earnings from fees on their assets under management. Fund holding Apollo Global Management (APO) generates 79% of its fee-related earnings from perpetual capital vehicles or long-lived strategies. Another holding, Brookfield Corporation (BN), generates 88% of its fee-related earnings from perpetual capital vehicles or long-lived strategies. Fees should be flowing into these two companies for a long time, regardless of any macroeconomic challenges.

Subscription Business Models: eDreams ODIGEO SA (OTCPK:EDDRF), the European online travel agency, generates most of its earnings from subscription fees. When COVID lockdowns ended in the summer of 2021, eDreams had 1 mm subscribers. Today, it has over 7 mm subscribers. eDreams grew its subscriber base despite the Omicron COVID variant, the invasion of Ukraine, war in the Middle East, and high global inflation, which was particularly acute in Europe. Europeans highly prioritize travel, and eDreams' subscription program helps their customers save money.

Geographic Advantaged Businesses: GFL Environmental, Inc. (GFL) is a municipal waste company. It is usually the dominant player in its markets, which gives it the best route density and the lowest cost. It also owns several landfills, each one typically a local monopoly. SECURE Waste Infrastructure Corp. (OTCPK:SECYF) primarily processes and disposes wastewater from oil production in Western Canada. Solids from the wastewater are disposed of in industrial landfills while the remaining liquids are placed in disposal wells. SECURE controls approximately 70% of the wastewater disposal capacity in Western Canada with minimal geographic overlap with competitors. Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) and LandBridge Company LLC (LB) have similar advantages with their wastewater disposal wells in the Permian Basin. UK-based SigmaRoc PLC mines limestone in the UK, the Nordics, and northern mainland Europe, holding the number one or two position in each of its markets. Limestone mines are like landfills, typically being the only operator in their geographic area. Furthermore, limestone is a key ingredient in steel manufacturing, which should benefit from the rebuilding of Europe's defense industry.

Secularly Growing Businesses: Chemed Corporation (CHE) owns two businesses: the acyclical Roto Rooter plumbing business and Vitas Healthcare. Vitas provides hospice and palliative care services, which are secularly growing industries. Vitas' growth is driven by an aging society that needs its services and by the need for the US government to displace expensive curative care that is inefficacious in the later years of life with compassionate palliative care. The demand for Vitas' services will occur regardless of the macroeconomic environment.

Taking Advantage of Market Dislocations: Companies with steady earnings streams can take actions that greatly benefit shareholders during times of stock price volatility through share repurchases. While most of the Fund's holdings repurchase shares, four of the holdings are buying back substantial amounts of stock. eDreams is buying stock every day near the daily limit allowed by their regulators. SECURE Waste Infrastructure Corp. bought back 19% of its stock in 2024 and is on pace to buy back another 8% in 2025. It would not surprise us if SECURE announces a substantial issuer bid soon and increases its 2025 buybacks well beyond the currently estimated 8%. Chemed and GFL are also buying back substantial amounts of stock.

While challenging economic times bring uncertainty, we are confident that the Fund's holdings can both weather the macroeconomic environment and benefit from stock price volatility. Furthermore, holding high-quality companies for the long term has proven to be a solid investment strategy. Thank you for partnering with us.

Your investment in the Ave Maria Focused Fund is appreciated.

data.title: Scheduling Linked To 10% Tariffs Causing Confusion, Angst For U.S. Importers
data.description:
Some US importers with long ocean transit times, including those who move cargo through transshipment hubs, will be on the hook for the 10% US tariffs now in place on non-Chinese goods even if those goods beat an April 5 load-by deadline to avoid the tariffs.

That’s because of a shorter-than-usual length of time set by the Trump administration encompassing the cutoff dates between when goods needed to depart their origin ports and arrive at destination ports.

And that scheduling quirk is causing consternation for importers who believed they had avoided the first salvo of tariffs by loading their cargo before the prescribed date and are now facing unexpected costs.

Don Pisano, president of American Coffee Corp., said shipments from Southeast Asia to US Gulf ports regularly take 60 days, longer than the gap between the “load-by” April 5 deadline set by the White House and the May 27 arrival date set by US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) in which the goods must be “entered for consumption.”

“It seems completely arbitrary and punitive,” Pisano told the Journal of Commerce.

Pisano pointed to another example on a different lane, a containerized shipment of coffee that left Brazil’s Port of Santos in late March on a service that went west through the Panama Canal, to Shanghai and then to the US West Coast.

“That’s not arriving until May 29, and we’ll have to pay $20,000 in tariffs because [the carrier] routed it through Shanghai,” he said.

Further, shippers whose goods were transshipped, with the final leg departing after April 5, will also be subject to the 10% US tariffs, even if the cargo shipped from origin prior to April 5. In many cases, the decision to transship is out of the control of importers.

Delay in ‘end-date’ guidance

Gabrielle Griffith, a director at BPE Global, a trade compliance consulting firm, said an end date for the in-transit exemption was not initially specified when the reciprocal tariffs were imposed April 2 through President Donald Trump’s executive order. The end-date guidance came two days later, on April 4, in a separate message in the Cargo Systems Messaging Service (CSMS), a channel CBP uses to communicate with the trade community.

Griffith said the April 4 message from CBP added the May 27 cutoff in order to, in the agency’s words, “prevent abuse of exemption,” and was reiterated by CBP on April 8.

Pisano’s contention is that most goods aiming to beat the April 5 deadline were loaded or on the water prior to either of those guidance messages being issued, as importers were racing to beat the deadline.

Griffith said the end-date provision aligns with prior CBP practices for tariff exclusions but differs from earlier executive orders issued under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEPPA) that lacked time limits.

The end date was not specified in Trump’s executive order, but CBP has some leeway to interpret the application of that order.

Transshipment confusion

The transshipment issue is potentially more vexing for importers, as CBP’s original guidance on April 4 wasn’t clear, according to a range of trade compliance experts. At issue is whether reciprocal tariffs would be exempted if goods departed their origin port before April 5, or if transshipped, departed the final leg of their ocean journey prior to April 5.

Guidance issued by CBP on May 15, which included a set of frequently asked questions, included details on how transshipment would impact tariff exemptions.

The guidance painted a scenario in which, prior to the cutoff date for the reciprocal tariff in-transit provision, US-bound cargo is loaded onto a vessel destined for a foreign port prior to shipment to the US. At that foreign port, after April 5, the cargo is transferred onto a different vessel destined for the US.

“The cargo in this scenario does not qualify for the in-transit exception for reciprocal tariffs because the US bound cargo was laden onto a vessel destined for the US after the cutoff date irrespective of when it departed from the original port of lading,” CBP wrote, responding to its own rhetorical question in the guidance.

A trade attorney who did not want to be identified told the Journal of Commerce that the May 15 guidance on transshipment is “inconsistent with previous guidance we received from CBP on the same question, so we are trying to reconcile this.”

The fact that guidance on transshipment came six weeks after the initial executive order will now impact importers for whom it wasn’t clear that such a routing wouldn’t be exempted, Pisano said.

“It took them six weeks to come up with the wrong conclusion,” he said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CrowdStrike's Renewed Growth Opportunity Has Been Overbought
data.description:
CRWD Offers Mixed Return Prospects At Current Inflated Levels

We previously covered CrowdStrike Holdings (NASDAQ:CRWD) in March 2025, discussing why its valuations remained extremely expensive despite the recent selloff, attributed to the projected deceleration in its growth profile in the low teens against the prior guidance of over ~80%.

Despite the stable TTM Dollar-Based Retention Rates and the increased cross-selling through the expanding module adoption rates, we had warned against chasing overvalued stocks then, worsened by the growing cybersecurity competition and the higher short interest volumes.

CRWD 1Y Stock Price

Since that Hold rating, the bulls have burned the bears indeed, as CRWD also charts an eye-watering +47.8% rally compared to the wider market at +5.5%, with a similar outperformance observed in its cybersecurity peers in varying degrees.

A similar exuberance has been observed in other enterprise SaaS stocks as well, with it is apparent that demand remains robust no matter the uncertainties arising from the macroeconomic headwinds and the tariff risks, as similarly reported by numerous market players in the recent Q1'25 earning season.

The same has been reported by CRWD in the FQ1'26 earnings call, with growing subscription revenues of $1.05B (+5% QoQ/ +20.6% YoY) and Annual Recurring Revenue of $4.44B (+4.7% QoQ/ +21.6% YoY).

Given the growing top-lines, it is apparent that the cybersecurity company has been able to increasingly cross-sell to its existing consumers, as observed in the expanding module adoption rates to 48% (inline QoQ/ +4 points YoY) for six or more, 32% (inline QoQ/ +4 points YoY) for seven or more, and 22% (+1 points QoQ/ NA YoY) for eight or more modules as of the latest quarter.

The same has been observed in CRWD's gross module retention rate of 97% and the dollar-based net retention rate "in line with our expectations" in the latest quarter, compared to 97%/ 112% in FY2025 and 98%/ 119% in FY2024, respectively.

These developments underscore why Falcon Flex has worked as intended in driving higher deal value for longer periods while triggering accelerated adoption/ spend/ platform stickiness, as similarly observed in the accelerating multi-year remaining performance obligations growth to $6.8B (+4.6% QoQ/ +44.6% YoY) compared to a year ago at $4.7B (-2.1% QoQ/ +42.4% YoY):

The average Flex customer deal size is greater than $1 million in ending ARR. Two, customers commit to longer durations. The average Flex subscription length is 31 months. And three, Flex customers adopt Falcon faster. More than 75% of Flex contracts are already deployed. (Seeking Alpha)

On the other hand, despite the relatively stable subscription gross margins of 80% (inline QoQ/ -1 points YoY), it goes without saying that these have been negated by CRWD's rather aggressive go-to-market strategy, as observed in the consistently deteriorating adj operating margins to 18.2% (-2.3 points QoQ/ -6.2 YoY).

It is apparent that their "strong competitive win rates and robust pipeline for the second half of fiscal year 2026" through the Falcon Flex strategy has not been bottom-line accretive, as similarly observed in the mixed adj EPS performance of $0.73 (-29.1% QoQ/ -7.5% YoY) in FQ1'26.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

While the management has optimistically raised their FY2027 adj operating margin guidance to at least 24% and Free Cash Flow margins to over 30% (compared to the current 25.3%), we can understand why the consensus have opted to lower their forward estimates, with CRWD expected to chart a decelerating top/ bottom-line growth profile at a CAGR of +22%/ +15.8% through FY2028 (CY2027).

This is compared to the original estimates of +28.9%/ +31% (a year ago) and the 4Y historical growth profile at a CAGR of +45.8%/ +95.3%, respectively, with it naturally underscoring its impacted bottom-line growth profile, despite the management's guidance of "sequential net new ARR growth rate to be at least double over what we saw from Q1 to Q2 in the prior fiscal year" and the "margin expansion in the second half of FY '26."

CRWD Valuations

This reason is also why we believe that CRWD remains overly expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 133.18x, up drastically from the sector median of 22.90x and the August 2024 bottom of 52.69x, albeit down from the 5Y mean of 346.87x.

When comparing its FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.49x to the sector median of 1.80x and the 4Y mean of 1.51x, it is undeniable that CRWD's decelerating growth profile comes at a relatively expensive cost.

A similar conclusion may also derive after comparing to its peers, including Sentinel (S) at 0.80x and Okta (OKTA) at 1.53x, aside from Fortinet (FTNT) at 2.99x, Palo Alto (PANW) at 2.92x, and Zscaler (ZS) at 5.01x, with it underscoring why the market may have gotten overly optimistic about the cybersecurity sector's prospects as observed in the greedy stock market sentiments.

Even if we are to use another metric typically used for mature SaaS companies, namely the cash from operations of $384.1M in FQ1'26 (inline YoY) and $1.38B in FY2025 (+17.9% YoY) along with the Free Cash Flow generation of $279.4M (-13.3% YoY) and $1.07B (+15% YoY), respectively, it is apparent that CRWD remains expensive at FWD Price/ Cash Flow valuations of 82.62x.

This is compared to the sector median of 19.22x and the 5Y mean of 76.85x, with it demonstrating its unattractive investment thesis at current inflated levels, albeit somewhat aided by the increasingly rich balance sheet at a net cash position of $3.87B (+8.1% QoQ/ +30.7% YoY).

So, Is CRWD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

CRWD 5Y Stock Price

For now, CRWD has charted new heights while running away from its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages, with it apparent that the bulls remain in the 'driver's seat.'

This reason is also why the stock is trading at a notable premium against our base-case fair value estimates of $234.50, despite the management's raised FY2026 adj EPS guidance to $3.50 at the midpoint (-10.9% YoY) and the cybersecurity peer group P/E mean of ~67x.

Based on the consensus' lowered FY2028 adj EPS estimates from $7.46 to $6.10, it is apparent that CRWD has already pulled forward nearly all of its upside potential to our base-case long-term price target of $499.80 as well.

Otherwise, a decent upside potential of +21.3% to our bull-case long-term price target of $567.90, based on the 1Y P/E mean of 93.11x, albeit with it remaining to be seen if the stock will be able to sustain its premium valuations pending the acceleration of its bottom-line growth profile ahead.

CRWD's Overbought Levels

If anything, CRWD is seemingly overbought at current levels as well, as observed in the increasingly elevated Money Flow Index and the heightened Williams %R Momentum indexes above, with it underscoring why the stock may potentially stagnate at current levels.

Otherwise, we may see the stock retrace to its prior July 2024 resistance levels of $400s ranges in the near-term, once market sentiments normalizes from current heights, with it implying a -16.1% downside from current levels.

This is worsened by CRWD's notably higher short interest volumes by +29.9% on a YoY basis, with the stock's recent gains potentially moderated by aggressive short sellers in an exuberant market environment.

As a result of the potential capital losses, we prefer to maintain our Hold rating here.

data.title: Webull: Strong Q1 Does Not Offset A 'Sell' Rating
data.description:
Two months after the SPAC of Webull Corporation (NASDAQ:BULL), the shares have consolidated, and the risk of owning this volatile stock has been reduced. Initially, the stock began trading at $16.00, and two trading days later, it reached a high of $59.21, which then rapidly declined to the point that two months later, the stock traded at $10.91, representing a -33.8% decrease from the initial trading price.

Although the company saw a notable financial improvement in Q1, I believe long-term net income trends, financial position, and valuation make me bearish on the stock of Webull, and in this analysis, I will explain my reasons to granting the stock a "sell" rating.

Business Overview

Webull is perhaps one of the closest competitors to Robinhood (HOOD). They were founded in 2016, and from my personal view, became highly popular during the pandemic, with popular financial influencers promoting them on YouTube with referral campaigns that were more appealing than the competition.

The broker offers investors an advanced trading platform with global quotes from more than 17 markets, including U.S. pre- and post-market hours. Providing trading solutions such as stocks, U.S. treasuries, ETFs, options, futures, access to the over-the-counter market, and margin trading. At the same time, they provide cash management tools for uninvested cash, recurring investing, IRAs, and money market funds.

Besides the United States, Webull has an international presence in countries such as Canada, the UK, China, Brazil, Mexico, Japan, Singapore, South Korea, and more. Yet, the vast majority of the revenues come from the United States.

Financial Trends in Q1

Over the past year, Webull experienced a notable operating leverage. In Q1, revenues grew 32% YoY to $117.4 million, while adjusted operating expenses remained fairly constant compared to a year ago, just rising 2.4%, ending at $88.7 million. And as a consequence, the adjusted operating profit expanded 22 percentage points from Q1 2024. Strong results like these are the ones that I like to see in companies. Nevertheless, the fact that adjusted expenses remained constant was mainly due to the significant decrease in marketing & branding expenses of -32.2% YoY. Also, this positive trend hasn't been the case over the past two fiscal years, and more of this will be discussed later in the analysis.

Revenue Drivers

As a predominantly commission-free broker, most of Webull's earnings are still derived from trading-related activities. For cash equity and equity options, they earn a commission from payment for order flow, where they send their orders to a market maker in exchange for a small fee. Nonetheless, for index options, they do charge $0.50 per contract, and if the number of contracts exceeds 500, they charge an oversized fee of $0.10 per contract.

In Q1 2025, Webull generated $81.7 million in trading-related revenues (72.4% of the total), with the majority coming from option revenue, followed by equity revenue, and platform and trading fees. Here, revenues grew 50% YoY, in tandem with a 45% YoY rise in customer assets.

The other revenue component is interest-related income, where Webull earns net interest income from lending customers cash, stock lending, and interest on margin financing. In Q1, this represented 33% of the business, with most of the income coming from lending clients' cash. Due to lower net interest margins resulting from an asset-sensitive balance sheet and lower interest rates, interest-related income declined by -4.3% YoY, which would have been significantly lower if customers' assets had not experienced a strong rise.

With the current landscape of higher inflation expectations from tariffs and a monetary policy where the market implies just two interest rate cuts in 2025, clients' cash as a revenue component will likely rise through the remainder of the year, as the company inboards new customers and new deposits continue rising the cash from customers.

Pipeline For Product Expansion

Webull is a broker that is experiencing strong growth. Three years ago, the company had 13.6 million registered accounts, and in Q1 2025, this number almost doubled to 24.1 million accounts. They operate in a monopolistic competition with a vast number of competitors offering similar solutions, and therefore, they do not have a strong moat compared to other market competitors. Yet, their strategy relies on entering global markets and expanding their products with solutions such as prediction markets, premium subscriptions, crypto trading, and the launch of a mobile app in Brazil, Mexico, and the Netherlands.

Risks

Although core KPIs, such as client assets, retention rates, and notional volumes, are increasing on a yearly basis, the company has experienced a slight revenue growth, and the bottom line has shown a negative trend. In 2022, the company experienced an adjusted net income gain of $91.602 million, which in 2023 declined to $35.481 million, and most recently in 2024, dropped to $9.894 million. This drop is mainly attributed to equity and option order flow rebates that passed from $278.981 million in FY 2022 to $197.070 million in FY 2024. However, as previously noted, when analyzing the Q1 results, there was a notable improvement in the bottom line compared to the same period last year, as revenues increased and expenses remained fairly constant.

From a short-term perspective, the company is doing well and growing its EPS sustainably. Yet, from a long-term perspective, considering the past three fiscal years, there is an underlying problem where the company is just able to experience a modest top-line growth, and higher expenses have caused the net income to fall substantially.

Valuation: An Overvalued Multiple

Finding a valuation method to value Webull is complex due to the following reasons.

  1. Their trailing net income of just $2.8 million, based on a market cap of more than $5 billion, will provide a multiple that is too high and unrealistic.
  2. Their shareholders' equity is negative by more than $2 billion, due to a significant amount of mezzanine equity, and therefore, multiples with book value are not possible to use.
  3. As a brokerage firm, they do not provide guidance, as most of their revenue is market-dependent.

Therefore, price-to-sales is perhaps the multiple to use in the valuation, and here, Webull has a PS ratio of 11.82x, which is higher than Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) and The Charles Schwab (SCHW), but remains lower than Robinhood. All in all, from an absolute perspective, I believe the multiple of 11.82x price to sales is overvalued due to the low net income margin, and the modest revenue growth experienced over the past three fiscal years, despite having a strong recovery in Q1.

Takeaway: Sell

Considering all the aspects, Webull is definitely not a strong company. Their negative shareholder equity is perhaps a red flag to an investment, when it comes from the funding side, and not from an effect of aggressive repurchases, as is the case with a company such as MSCI (MSCI). At the same time, although they have robust trading tools for self-directed retail investors, the competition is high, and they do not have a strong moat. Although the last quarter was appealing and KPIs continue to experience a positive trend, combined with the valuation, this is not enough. Therefore, I will rate the shares of Webull as a "sell."
